statusLine: EU fuel reserves remain under strain in June 2026, with 15 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel type below the 90-day mandatory minimum, even as bloc-wide averages stay above threshold.
fullAnalysis:
As of mid-June 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position sits at a 'watch' level, with average reserves of 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While petrol stocks across the bloc nominally exceed the 90-day mandatory minimum, both diesel and jet fuel averages fall short — a structural vulnerability that reflects ongoing drawdowns from strategic reserves following late-February supply disruptions tied to Middle East escalation. Brent crude has climbed 9.8% since that escalation began, settling at $79.6 per barrel as of June 16, adding cost pressure to any restocking efforts.

The country-level picture is considerably more concerning than the EU averages suggest. A total of 15 of 27 member states are in a critical status for at least one fuel type, meaning stocks fall well below the 90-day threshold. The most exposed countries are Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal, all of which are critical across all three fuel categories. Romania's diesel position — at just 30.2 days — is the lowest single-fuel reading in the dataset. Jet fuel is a particular weakness across the bloc: 18 of 27 countries hold jet fuel stocks at critical levels, including major aviation hubs such as France (65.0 days), Germany (59.9 days), Italy (61.5 days), and the Netherlands (63.2 days). This points to a sector-specific supply chain stress that warrants close monitoring ahead of peak summer travel season.

A smaller group of member states presents a more reassuring picture. Estonia (petrol: 208.2d, diesel: 124.3d, jet fuel: 320.0d), Ireland, Latvia, and Finland hold robust stocks across most categories, though Finland's jet fuel position (31.9 days) is a notable outlier. Slovenia's jet fuel reading of 365 days stands out as exceptionally high and may reflect methodological differences in how net imports are calculated for small, landlocked economies. On the price side, EU average petrol stands at €1.786/L and diesel at €1.804/L, with Denmark and the Netherlands at the high end and Malta and Poland at the low end — a spread that partly reflects differing tax regimes and proximity to refining infrastructure rather than supply stress alone.

The combination of below-minimum diesel and jet fuel averages at the EU level, critical stock positions in 15 of 27 member states, a Brent price trend that makes restocking more expensive, and confirmed strategic reserve releases creates a picture of a fuel security environment that is meaningfully under pressure. Policymakers should pay particular attention to the Central and Eastern European corridor — Poland, Romania, Austria, Slovakia, and Hungary — where diesel deficits are acute and geographic dependence on specific supply routes remains elevated.
keyPoints: EU diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) averages both fall below the mandatory 90-day minimum, while petrol (95.6 days) narrowly exceeds it — the bloc-wide position is under strain, not comfortable.
15 of 27 member states are at critical status for at least one fuel type; Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal are critical across all three fuel categories.

Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: 18 of 27 countries hold jet fuel stocks at critical levels, including France, Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands — a significant concern ahead of peak summer aviation demand.
Romania holds the lowest single-fuel reading in the dataset at 30.2 days of diesel, while Poland's jet fuel position (22.5 days) and Portugal's (21.2 days) are also severely depleted.
Brent crude has risen 9.8% since the late-February Middle East escalation to $79.6/barrel, raising the cost of replenishing stocks that have already been partially drawn down from strategic reserves.
